title: Today's AI Brief: Google's Gemini Spark and Cisco's AI Leap
summary: Google's 24/7 AI assistant Gemini Spark and Cisco's partnership with OpenAI make waves in AI
imageAlt: Image from source article for I put Google’s 24/7 AI assistant Gemini Spark to work, and it’s actually pretty useful
imageCaption: I put Google’s 24/7 AI assistant Gemini Spark to work, and it’s actually pretty useful
imageSourceTitle: I put Google’s 24/7 AI assistant Gemini Spark to work, and it’s actually pretty useful

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI briefing. I've been digging into some fresh developments that are worth sharing. First up, I've been testing Google's new 24/7 AI assistant called Gemini Spark. I have to say, it's been a surprisingly useful tool for getting my digital life in order. From what I've seen, it's great at summarizing long articles and helping with online tasks.

Gemini Spark is designed to help you navigate your online tasks, summarize long reads, and basically make your digital life easier. For instance, it can summarize articles you don't have time to read, which is perfect for someone like me who's always on the go. The idea here is to have a constant assistant that can chip in and help with tasks, making it a potentially game-changing tool for productivity. I've found it really helpful for getting through my inbox and staying on top of things.
Cisco and OpenAI: Engineering with AI

On another front, Cisco and OpenAI are teaming up to redefine enterprise engineering with something called Codex. This partnership aims to help Cisco scale AI-native development, speed up AI defense work, and automate fixing defects. Essentially, they're looking to make engineering more efficient and effective with AI, which could have significant implications for how companies approach software development and maintenance. It's exciting to see how AI is being used to improve the way we work.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
